As a hairdresser, I've always been a people person, but with the rise of social media, it's become increasingly difficult to separate fact from fiction. I mean, have you seen those viral videos that claim to have the 'secret' to perfect hair? It's all just a bunch of misinformation, if you ask me. And don't even get me started on those 'influencers' who seem to have the perfect life, but are actually just peddling products that don't even work. It's enough to make a person anxious, let me tell you. But, I've learned a thing or two about how to manage and deal with this fake content. First of all, I try to limit my time on social media as much as possible. I mean, I know it's hard to resist the temptation of scrolling through my feeds, but I've come to realize that it's just a waste of time. And secondly, I try to be more discerning about what I read and share. I ask myself, 'Is this really true?' or 'Is this just a sales pitch?' And if I'm not sure, I don't share it. It's that simple. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As someone who's been around for a while and has spent countless hours on social media, I've got to say that dealing with fake content has become a real concern for me. As a hairdresser, I've always been a people person, but with the rise of social media, it's become increasingly difficult to separate fact from fiction. I mean, have you seen those viral videos that claim to have the 'secret' to perfect hair? It's all just a bunch of misinformation, if you ask me. And don't even get me started on those 'influencers' who seem to have the perfect life, but are actually just peddling products that don't even work. It's enough to make a person anxious, let me tell you. But, I've learned a thing or two about how to manage and deal with this fake content. First of all, I try to limit my time on social media as much as possible. I mean, I know it's hard to resist the temptation of scrolling through my feeds, but I've come to realize that it's just a waste of time. And secondly, I try to be more discerning about what I read and share. I ask myself, 'Is this really true?' or 'Is this just a sales pitch?' And if I'm not sure, I don't share it. It's that simple. And let me tell you, it's made a world of difference. I feel less anxious, and I'm actually able to focus on the things that matter most to me - my family, my friends, and my garden. Knitting's a close second, of course!
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've come to realize that fake content on social media is a real concern for me, and I've developed a few strategies to deal with it. Firstly, I limit my time on social media to reduce my exposure to misinformation. Secondly, I'm more discerning about what I read and share, asking myself if the content is true or just a sales pitch. This has helped me feel less anxious and more focused on the things that matter most to me.
